fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund

allocations.rationale: Core holding. Tracks Nifty Midcap 150 (next 50-150 large stocks after Nifty 50). Lowest annual fee at 0.20%, broad mid-cap diversification. Captures India's growth engine—companies sized ₹500Cr–₹5,000Cr growing faster than large-caps over 20 years. 5-year CAGR 18.7%.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Smallcap 250 Index Fund

allocations.rationale: Satellite holding. Tracks India's 200-250 small-cap companies. 0.20% annual fee. Holds investor in the next wave of growth. Higher volatility but 5-year CAGR 16.9%. 20-year horizon enables riding out volatility; small-cap drawdowns of 30-40% are normal but recoveries are potent. Caps small-cap concentration risk through diversification.

Stability anchor for lumpsum deployment when ashore. 1.00% annual fee justified by consistent 17.7% 5-year CAGR and 4.3% outperformance alpha. Active large-cap manager adds resilience during downturns. Lumpsum-friendly anchor when deploying ₹1-2L every 4-6 months.

plan.setup_phase:
Step 1: Open Direct Accounts (this month) using Kuvera, Groww, or ET Money to invest directly (no middleman fees). Open accounts under own name; enable e-SIP for ₹60k/month. Set up auto-debit from salary account.

Step 2: Deploy Existing Corpus (₹20 L lumpsum) immediately: ₹12 L into Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 (60% of corpus), ₹5 L into Motilal Oswal Nifty Smallcap 250 (25% of corpus), ₹3 L into Nippon India Large Cap Fund (15% of corpus). Deploy in one shot—don't try to time.

Step 3: Set up Monthly SIP (₹73,400/month) with ₹44,000 to Nifty Midcap 150, ₹18,400 to Nifty Smallcap 250, ₹11,000 to Nippon India Large Cap. Automate via e-SIP set for 20th of every month (after salary). Do NOT pause this SIP.

Step 4: Lumpsum Deployment Cadence (When Ashore) every 4-6 months when docking, deploy ₹2-2.5 L lumpsum: ₹1.3 L to Nifty Midcap 150 (60%), ₹0.55 L to Nifty Smallcap 250 (25%), ₹0.33 L to Nippon India Large Cap Fund (15%). This maintains target allocation while earning and accumulating.

Step 5: Track on Single Dashboard with all three funds on Groww (free). Set calendar reminders: quarterly glance only (don't act), annual deep review (December).
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Are SIPs running? Check NAVs are growing. If all running smoothly, do nothing. If any SIP failed, restart immediately.

End of Year 1: Compare 3 funds' 1-year returns to benchmarks (Nifty Midcap 150, Nifty Smallcap 250). Are you tracking the index with low drag? If within 1% of index, great, do nothing. If lagging badly, verify fund tickers for error.
End of Year 3: Test portfolio growth. (₹20L initial + ₹26.4L SIPs so far) × 1.20 ≈ ₹55.7L target. If within 10% of target, on track. If more than 10% below, review allocation (likely market cycles).
Annual (December): REBALANCE if drift exceeds 5%. Check actual allocation vs target (60/25/15 split). If drift <5%, hold. If drift >5%, rebalance by moving money between funds, not selling.

Every 5 years: If returns significantly underperform category (e.g., mid-cap index returns <15% CAGR when expectation is 18%), investigate. Usually market cycles explain it. If structural fund problem, flag for replacement.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines:
Rebalance once per calendar year (typically December after annual bonus/lumpsum). Check actual allocation vs target of 60% mid-cap / 25% small-cap / 15% large-cap.

Example: If mid-cap has grown to 65% of portfolio (target 60%) and small-cap to 22% (target 25%), drift exceeds 5% threshold. Action: Move funds from outperformer (mid-cap) to underperformer (small-cap) to restore 60/25/15 split. Execute by selling ₹2L of mid-cap index and buying ₹2L of small-cap index.

Rebalancing forces disciplined "sell high, buy low" mechanical discipline, prevents one segment from taking over and hiding risk. Frequency: Once per calendar year only. Not quarterly, not monthly. Direct plan has no fees; long-term gains tax minimal. This is surveillance-driven rebalancing, not active trading.
plan.rationale:
Investor is 40 years old with 20-year horizon to retirement (age 60), ₹20 L existing corpus, ₹60,000/month SIP capacity, aggressive risk appetite in new tax regime (no 80C benefit). Goal: ₹8 Cr by age 60 for retirement, house purchase, and daughter's education abroad.

Plan follows a core-satellite index strategy: stable, low-cost index foundation plus satellite small-cap position for growth. 100% equities appropriate given long horizon and aggressive risk appetite. All equity-taxed at 12.5% long-term capital gains tax (on gains above ₹1.25 L held >1 year) instead of higher marginal rates.

Index funds chosen for low expense ratios (0.20% vs typical 1%+ active funds), which compounds to ₹50+ lakhs extra over 20 years. Mid-cap (60%) and small-cap (25%) tilt captures India's growth engine where companies sized ₹500Cr–₹5,000Cr grow 16-20% long-term vs 10-12% for large-caps. Large-cap active fund (15%) provides stability anchor and lumpsum deployment flexibility matching investor's life pattern (ashore deployments every 4-6 months, SIP when at sea).

New tax regime makes ELSS irrelevant. Pure equity index funds optimal. Monthly SIP provides rupee cost averaging; lumpsum deployments while ashore maintain growth trajectory. 20-year horizon makes volatility manageable and provides recovery time for cycles.
risks: Markets can drop 30-40% in a bad year. Example: ₹50L portfolio becomes ₹30-35L overnight. This will happen once or twice in 20 years. Mitigation: Historical recovery within 2-3 years if investor stays invested and keeps SIP running. Panic-selling creates permanent loss.
Small-cap volatility is real. Small-cap funds swing ±25% in bad years. That 25% of portfolio magnifies moves. This is intentional for growth but expect sleepless nights every 5-7 years.

Concentration risk if markets crash. If India's growth slows (political instability, global recession), mid/small-caps hit hardest. 85% portfolio in these segments means harder decline than benchmark. Mitigation: 20-year horizon. Downturns pass. 2008 crash recovered fully by 2012 with 300%+ gains by 2024.
Lumpsum timing risk. Deploying ₹2L right before 30% crash loses ₹0.6L on paper. Mitigation: 20-year investor horizon makes dip just average cost reduction, not disaster.
Personal life changes. Early retirement, income loss, emergency fund needs break this plan. Mitigation: Build separate 6-month emergency fund (liquid, low-return) before deploying ₹20L+ lumpsum.

Sequence of returns risk at retirement. Markets crashing 2-3 years before age 60 turns ₹8Cr into ₹5Cr with no 2-year recovery window. Mitigation: Around age 55, rebalance 60% into safer debt funds to lock in gains.
plan.disclaimer: This is educational research and not certified financial advice. Author is not a SEBI-registered advisor. Consult a registered financial planner before deploying ₹20L+ or making tax decisions. Tax treatment can change with policy. Verify all fund details on SEBI website or brokerage platform before investing. For research/educational purposes only.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year to match salary growth

allocation_schedule.trigger: Starting allocation at age 40, 20-year horizon. 100% equities appropriate given long time horizon and aggressive risk appetite.
Age 55: Begin gradual shift to lock in gains. Rebalance 60% of portfolio into safer debt funds as retirement approaches within 5 years.
Age 60 (Retirement): Conservative allocation to mitigate sequence of returns risk. Reduce equity exposure, increase debt for stability in withdrawal phase.

aps.reasoning:
This is a **highly passive, low-cost, long-term index-driven investment plan** with exceptionally strong alignment to passive investing principles.

**passive_instrument_fraction: 0.85**
Of the 100% equity allocation, 85% (₹20L: ₹12L Nifty Midcap 150 + ₹5.5L Nifty Smallcap 250 / total ₹20L) flows directly to passive broad-market index funds. The remaining 15% goes to Nippon India Large Cap Fund, an actively managed large-cap equity fund. The plan explicitly states it is a "core-satellite index strategy," with index funds as the dominant foundation. Since debt/hybrid/gold are absent, the equity sleeve scoring is unambiguous: 85% passive + 15% active = 0.85. This is a strong passive orientation.

**turnover_score: 0.88**
The plan mandates **annual rebalancing only** ("once per calendar year, typically December") with a 5% drift threshold before action. Review checkpoints are quarterly glances (minimal action) and annual deep reviews. There is explicit guidance against frequent tactical shifts: "Not quarterly, not monthly." The monthly SIP is mechanical and not adjusted tactically. This is classic buy-and-hold discipline. The only regular portfolio activity is one rebalance per year, which is mechanical and not discretionary. Score: 0.88 (very passive, with minimal turnover).

**cost_emphasis_score: 0.92**
Cost minimization is **frontline and dominant** throughout the plan. Key quotes: "Lowest annual fee at 0.20%," "Index funds chosen for low expense ratios (0.20% vs typical 1%+ active funds), which compounds to ₹50+ lakhs extra over 20 years," "Direct plan has no fees," "all equity-taxed at 12.5% long-term capital gains tax." The plan explicitly avoids ELSS ("New tax regime makes ELSS irrelevant"). The large-cap fund (1.0% ER) is retained for "stability anchor and lumpsum deployment flexibility," not for alpha; even this justification is pragmatic, not conviction-driven. Cost is clearly the **primary decision criterion**, not secondary. Score: 0.92 (cost is paramount).

**research_vs_cost_score: 0.90**
There is **no stock-level, sector-level, or fund-manager research** in this plan. The plan states: "Pure equity index funds optimal," and selects funds based on: (1) index tracking (Nifty Midcap 150, Nifty Smallcap 250), (2) low fees (0.20%), and (3) broad diversification ("broad mid-cap diversification," "next 200-250 small-cap companies"). No company analysis, no competitive moat assessment, no manager track-record deep dives (the Nippon fund is selected for pragmatic lumpsum flexibility, not manager skill conviction). The plan relies on market-cap-weighted indices. Score: 0.90 (minimal research, pure market exposure).

**time_horizon_alignment_score: 0.95**
The plan is **explicitly designed for a 20-year, patient, long-term horizon**. The investor is 40 years old targeting retirement at 60. The setup is "buy, hold, and SIP for 20 years with minimal intervention." The plan acknowledges volatility ("30-40% drops once or twice in 20 years") and frames it as **manageable within the long horizon**: "20-year investor horizon makes dip just average cost reduction, not disaster," "Historical recovery within 2-3 years if investor stays invested." At year 15, the plan begins a **gradual, multi-year de-risking phase** (85% equity, 15% debt), not an abrupt shift, showing respect for time-horizon consistency. The only short-term tactical element is the lumpsum deployment cadence every 4-6 months tied to the investor's ashore/at-sea work pattern, but this is **deterministic and pre-planned**, not opportunistic. Score: 0.95 (textbook long-term, patient, decades-long strategy).

**portfolio_activeness_score: 0.82**
- **Mid-cap Index Fund (60%, ₹12L)**: Passive index, tracking Nifty Midcap 150. No alpha data provided, but category is "Index Fund." Beta ≈ 1.0 by design. Tracking error should be <1%. **Contributes 0.98 passiveness.**
- **Small-cap Index Fund (25%, ₹5.5L)**: Passive index, tracking Nifty Smallcap 250. Same logic. **Contributes 0.98 passiveness.**
- **Nippon India Large Cap Fund (15%, ₹3.3L)**: Actively managed. Alpha = 4.3% (documented outperformance). However, no beta or tracking error provided. Given it's a large-cap fund and the alpha is significant (4.3%), this suggests genuine active management with some concentration or skillful stock selection. Without tracking error data, I infer this is a genuinely active fund (not a closet indexer), likely with tracking error 5-8% and beta slightly >1.0. **Contributes ~0.30 passiveness** (30% passive behavior, 70% active).

Weighted portfolio passiveness: (60% × 0.98) + (25% × 0.98) + (15% × 0.30) = 0.588 + 0.245 + 0.045 = 0.878 ≈ **0.88 pure passivity score**.

However, the **portfolio_activeness_score** framework asks: "Does the recommended portfolio reflect genuine active conviction or index-like passive exposure?" The large-cap fund is a 15% position with 4.3% alpha and is selected for pragmatic reasons (lumpsum flexibility, stability anchor), not for conviction-driven active bets or alpha-chasing. The satellite is small. The core (85%) is pure index. The portfolio **overall behaves like 85% passive + modest active satellite**, which is index-like with a small hedge. Score: **0.82** (predominantly passive with a small active satellite; not truly passive, but very index-like in behavior).

**Composite APS: (0.85 + 0.88 + 0.92 + 0.90 + 0.95 + 0.82) / 6 = 0.8867 ≈ 0.89 (Highly Passive)**

---

### Summary
This plan scores **0.89 on the Active-Passive spectrum**, placing it in the **highly passive category**. It exhibits:
✓ 85% passive equity instruments (index funds)
✓ Annual-only rebalancing with mechanical discipline
✓ Cost minimization as the **primary selection criterion**
✓ No security-level or manager-level research; pure market exposure
✓ 20-year buy-and-hold time horizon with minimal tactical moves
✓ Portfolio-weighted mix of 85% passive + 15% active satellite

This is an **exemplary passive, low-cost, long-term index-based wealth-building plan** appropriate for a patient, long-horizon investor comfortable with index-level returns and equity volatility.

pqs.reasoning:
GOAL ALIGNMENT (0.92): Excellent alignment with investor's aggressive profile, 20-year horizon, and lumpsum+SIP investment pattern. The plan directly addresses his merchant navy lifestyle (4-6 month lumpsum deployments when ashore, SIP when at sea) and targets an 8 Cr corpus by retirement through 100% equity allocation. Projected returns (18.1% base, 22.1% bull, 14.1% bear CAGR) are well-reasoned and support the goal, though the bull-case NAV (₹158B) appears to have a typo/data error (should be ₹15.8Cr, not ₹158Cr in original). Minor deduction for this apparent error and lack of explicit coverage of the house-purchase and daughter's education goals (though large corpus naturally funds these).

DIVERSIFICATION (0.78): Good but not excellent. The plan holds only 3 funds from 2 fund houses (Motilal Oswal and Nippon India), concentrating risk on these managers. Within equities, the 60/25/15 split between mid-cap / small-cap / large-cap is sensible and provides reasonable segment diversification. However, the plan lacks: (a) large-cap index fund (relying only on active Nippon India), (b) international equity exposure (given daughter's education abroad goal and 20-year horizon, a 5-10% international allocation could hedge INR depreciation risk and diversify geography), and (c) fund-house diversification (only 2 houses). The index-heavy approach is tax-efficient but concentrates single-track-record risk on Nifty indices and Motilal Oswal execution.

RISK_RETURN_APPROPRIATENESS (0.88): Well-calibrated for an aggressive investor with a 20-year horizon. The plan correctly identifies mid/small-cap as India's growth engine (16-20% LT returns vs 10-12% large-cap) and allocates 85% there. The risk warnings are thorough (30-40% drawdowns, sequence-of-returns risk) and realistic. Projected CAGR of 18.1% is justifiable given 5-year performance (Midcap 18.7%, Smallcap 16.9%) and appropriate for a young aggressive investor. The allocation schedule (shift to 85/15 at age 55, 60/40 at age 60) shows forward-thinking risk management. Minor deduction: The plan doesn't explicitly quantify the volatility (likely 15-20% annual swing) or provide a Monte Carlo analysis of downside scenarios, which would strengthen confidence in "appropriate" risk level.

INTERNAL_CONSISTENCY (0.85): The plan is largely consistent. Rationale, allocations, and execution steps align well. The core-satellite index strategy is clearly stated and supported throughout. The rebalancing logic (annual, 5% drift threshold, restore 60/25/15 split) is practical and avoids overtrade. However, minor inconsistencies: (a) The projected returns table has apparent magnitude errors (₹79Cr for base case is off by ~1 order of magnitude given inputs; should verify calculations), (b) the plan says "Do NOT pause this SIP" but doesn't address what happens if the investor's earnings drop or life circumstances change, and (c) the shift to debt allocation at years 15 and 20 is flagged as a future trigger but isn't integrated into the current 100% equity plan—a brief note on how/when to execute this would strengthen consistency.

TAX_EFFICIENCY (0.95): Excellent. The plan correctly identifies the investor is in new_regime (no 80C benefit) and wisely omits ELSS entirely. Instead, it recommends 100% equities (which receive preferential 12.5% LTCG tax on gains >₹1.25L held >1yr for funds ≥65% equity), avoiding slab-taxed debt/liquid/overnight funds that would be taxed at his marginal rate. The rationale explicitly states "All equity-taxed at 12.5% long-term capital gains tax instead of higher marginal rates," showing clear tax awareness. The plan notes ₹50+ lakhs tax savings over 20 years from low expense ratios, which compounds the tax benefit. Direct plan recommendation (vs regular) also avoids distributor fees. Minor deduction: The plan doesn't quantify the exact LTCG tax impact (e.g., ₹3Cr corpus with ₹5Cr gains = ₹3.75L LTCG at 12.5% = ₹46.9K tax vs hypothetical debt slab-tax), which would make the tax case even more concrete.
